title: The Sick Family
scenario: On a cold Christmas Eve evening, three members of the same family arrive at the ER within a short period of time. Earlier that night, they had eaten dinner together at home. All report headache, dizziness, nausea, and fatigue. Flu is circulating in the community, and at first the symptoms seem routine.

cluesRound1.title: Check flu exposure history
cluesRound1.result: Two family members report coworkers recently had the flu.

cluesRound1.title: Ask what they ate
cluesRound1.result: All three ate the same home-cooked dinner earlier in the evening. Steak and lobster.

cluesRound1.title: Ask about home environment
cluesRound1.result: They say they've been staying inside most of the day with the windows closed because of the cold.

cluesRound2.title: Check their temperature for possible fever
cluesRound2.result: None of the patients have a fever.

cluesRound2.title: Ask whether they have eaten lobster before
cluesRound2.result: Lobster is a family tradition they have every Christmas Eve.

cluesRound2.title: Ask whether anything in the home changed recently
cluesRound2.result: The parents mention they started using a new space heater this week because of the cold.

decisions.text: Treat for likely food poisoning, start IV fluids, and continue monitoring.
Treat for likely flu given the recent exposure history.
Administer oxygen and investigate a possible environmental exposure in the home.

expertAction: Keep the family for evaluation and immediately investigate carbon monoxide exposure.
expertExplanation: Multiple people from the same home developing identical symptoms at the same time strongly suggests an environmental cause. Carbon monoxide poisoning often presents with headache, dizziness, and nausea and can easily be mistaken for flu or food poisoning.
lesson: When several patients from one location get sick together, think environmental exposure before common illness.

outcomes.A: They are treated for food poisoning while the true cause remains active in the home. Overnight, their condition worsens dangerously.
outcomes.B: They are discharged with a flu diagnosis while the real hazard remains active in their home. Overnight, their condition worsens dangerously.
outcomes.C: The environmental cause is identified, the family receives the right treatment, and further exposure is prevented.
